critique.summary: The article describes the author's experience reimplementing the 3dfx Voodoo 1 graphics chip on an FPGA using modern RTL tools like SpinalHDL and conetrace. It highlights the complexity of the fixed-function Voodoo architecture, the challenges of accurately modeling its register behaviors and pipeline timing, and how modern tools enabled a more manageable and precise design and debugging process.
mainPoints: The 3dfx Voodoo 1 GPU is a fixed-function graphics chip with complex hardwired rendering behaviors rather than programmable shaders.
Register writes on the Voodoo have different timing behaviors to avoid rendering corruption due to pipeline concurrency.
The author categorized Voodoo registers into four types based on their timing semantics and encoded these directly in SpinalHDL.
Debugging subtle rendering bugs required detailed tracing of pixel data through the pipeline, which was facilitated by the netlist-aware conetrace tool.
The main rendering artifacts were caused by small hardware-accuracy mismatches in precision, rounding, and blending rather than memory hazards.
Modern RTL tools like SpinalHDL and conetrace allowed the author to represent architectural intent clearly and debug the design effectively as a single person.
critique.truthfulness: The article provides a detailed and technically plausible account of reimplementing the 3dfx Voodoo 1 GPU on FPGA using modern hardware description and debugging tools. The descriptions of the Voodoo's fixed-function pipeline, register semantics, and rendering pipeline challenges align with known technical details about the original hardware. The use of SpinalHDL and conetrace for register modeling and debugging is consistent with current FPGA development practices. While the article is a first-person technical report without external citations, the claims about the hardware and tools are credible and consistent with publicly available information about the Voodoo 1 and modern RTL methodologies.
considerations: The article does not provide external references or comparisons to other FPGA reimplementations or emulators of the Voodoo 1 for validation.
It focuses on the author's specific approach and tools, so alternative methods or challenges are not discussed.
The article assumes familiarity with hardware design concepts and does not explain basic terminology for general audiences.
Performance metrics or compatibility testing results compared to original hardware or software emulators are not included.
The impact of this reimplementation on practical use cases, such as gaming or software compatibility, is not addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around nostalgia and technical appreciation for the 3dfx Voodoo graphics cards and the FPGA recreation project. Commenters share personal memories, technical critiques, and mixed feelings about the modern blog presentation and project design choices.

comments.author: ashwinnair99
comments.text: The Voodoo cards had no right to look as good as they did for their time. Someone rebuilding one from scratch is exactly the kind of project HN was made for.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately captures the impressive quality of the original Voodoo cards and the relevance of the FPGA project to the Hacker News community.

comments.author: VonTum
comments.text:
I find it odd the author adds all these extra semantics to their input registers, rather than keeping the FIFOs, "drain + FIFOs", "float to fixed point converting register", etc as separate components, separate from the task of being memory mapped registers. The central problem they were running into was one where they let the external controller asynchronously change state in the middle of the compute unit using it.

I'm noting down this conetrace for the future though, seems like a useful tool, and they seem to be doing a closed beta of sorts.
comments.analysis: Provides a detailed technical critique of the FPGA design approach, showing understanding of hardware design challenges and offering useful insights.

comments.author: ekelsen
comments.text: The project is cool, but the LLM generated blog bothers my brain.
comments.analysis: Expresses a subjective reaction to the blog style, which may resonate with some readers but does not address technical content.

comments.author: sejje
comments.text:
My first video card.

Getting it working in linux in ~1999 was really not easy, especially for a teenager with no linux experience.

My networking card wasn't working either, so I had to run to a friend's house for dial-up internet access, searching for help on Altavista.

Very cool project. Way above my head, still!
comments.analysis: Shares a personal anecdote that adds nostalgic context and reflects the challenges of early Linux hardware support.

comments.author: 12_throw_away
comments.text:
I have such fond memories of my old Voodoo card. Surprised how much nostalgia those pictures evoked - its rendering really had a unique look this that (LLM-generated?) FPGA captured quite well.

IIRC, it was a gigantic (for the time) beast that barely fit in my chassis - BUT it had great driver support for ppc32/macos9 (which was already on its way out), and actually kept my machine going for longer than it had any right to.

And then, like a month after I bought it, NVidia bought 3dfx and immediately stopped supporting the drivers, leaving me with an extremely performant paperweight when I finally upgraded my machine. Thanks Jensen.
comments.analysis: Combines nostalgia with a positive assessment of the FPGA project’s visual fidelity, though the LLM mention is speculative.

comments.author: TapamN
comments.text:
Oof. The gamma on that screenshot.

If you want to see what it's supposed to look like, copy the screenshot into GIMP, go into "Color, Levels" and in the "Input Levels" section, there should be a textbox+spinner with a "1.00". Set that to 0.45.
comments.analysis: Offers a practical tip to correct the gamma issue in the screenshot, demonstrating useful knowledge about image editing.

comments.author: Tsiklon
comments.text: Tangentially related, that screenshot of Screamer 2 caught me off guard completely, I loved that game to death, and I feel I was the only one of my friends to have played it. Tremendous handling model and superb music.
comments.analysis: Shares a nostalgic personal memory related to a game shown in the screenshot, adding color but little technical insight.

comments.author: SilverElfin
comments.text: I love the names and branding of that era. Technology today is far more advanced but it doesn’t have that same excitement for consumers.
comments.analysis: Reflects on the cultural and emotional impact of branding from the era, which is a subjective but relevant observation.

comments.author: bob1029
comments.text:
I miss the box art more than the actual GPUs.

https://lockbooks.net/pages/overclocked-launch
comments.analysis: A brief nostalgic comment that adds little to the technical or substantive discussion.
